revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23993722414|commit triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
[[en:Iwakiyama Shrine]][[fr:Iwakiyama-jinja]][[ja:岩木山神社]][[ko:이와키 산 신사]][[mr:इवाकियामा जिंजा]][[zh:岩木山神社]] {{Infobox religious building | name = Iwakiyama Jinja
岩木山神社 | image = File:Iwakiyama Shrine haiden2.jpeg | caption = [[Heiden (Shinto)|Heiden]] of Iwakiyama Jinja | map_type = Japan Aomori Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|40.61606111|N|140.36545556|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|qid=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}} | founded_by = | established = 780 | date_destroyed = | location = Aomori-ken, Hirosakishi, Hyakuzawa | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = | location = {{ill|Hyakuzawa|qid=Q11580777|1=Hyakuzawa}} }} {{nihongo|'''Iwakiyama Shrine'''|岩木山神社|Iwakiyama jinja}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} in the city of [[:en:Hirosaki, Aomori|Hirosaki]] in {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|en|Aomori Prefecture|qid=Q71699|lt=Aomori Prefecture|1=Aomori Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former {{ill|Hirosaki Domain|en|Hirosaki Domain|qid=Q903555|lt=Tsugaru Domain|1=Hirosaki Domain}}. All of {{ill|Mount Iwaki|en|Mount Iwaki|qid=Q713857|lt=Mount Iwaki|1=Mount Iwaki}} is considered to be a portion of the shrine. The main festival of the shrine, the ''Oyama-sankei'', features a parade from the shrine to the top of the mountain, and is held annually at the time of the [[:en:Autumn equinox (Northern Hemisphere)|autumn equinox]]. The pilgrims carry colorful banners and are accompanied by traditional drums and flutes Plutschow. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Iwakiyama Shrine is the {{nihongo|'''[[Ōkuninushi]]'''| 大国主}}, referred to here as {{nihongo|'''Utsushikuniotama-no-kami'''|顕国魂神}}. Other ''kami'' include {{nihongo|'''Tatsubihime-no-kami'''|多都比姫神}}, {{nihongo|'''Ōhoyamatsum'''|大山祇神}}, {{nihongo|'''Sakanoue no Karitamaro-no-mikoto'''|坂上刈田麿|}}, {{nihongo|'''Ukanome-no-mikoto'''|宇賀能賣神}}, and {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Oyamakui no Kami|en|Oyamakui no Kami|qid=Q10935105|lt=Oyamakui no Kami|1=Oyamakui no Kami}}'''|大山咋神}}. ==History== The foundation of the Iwakiyama Shrine predates the historical period, and Mount Iwaki was a holy mountain for the local {{ill|Emishi|en|Emishi|qid=Q932574|lt=Emishi|1=Emishi}} tribes. Per shrine tradition, the shrine was established on the summit of Mount Iwaki in the year 780. It was rebuilt by the folk-hero [[Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] in the year 800 and dedicated to his father {{ill|Sakanoue no Karitamaro|en|Sakanoue no Karitamaro|qid=Q3137722|lt=Sakanoue no Karitamaro|1=Sakanoue no Karitamaro}}. Several subsidiary shrines were built around the base of the mountain in an area called the {{nihongo|Tokoshinai|十腰内||}} by the local inhabitants. One of these subsidiary shrines to the southeast of the mountain developed into the [[Shingon]] sect {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|qid=Q748|lt=Buddhist|1=Buddhism}} temple of {{nihongo|Hyakutaku-ji|百沢寺|}} in 1091, and became the predecessor of the present shrine. The three main peaks on Mount Iwaki were identified with the Buddhist deities of [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]], [[:en:Yakushi Nyorai|Yakushi Nyorai]] and [[:en:Kannon Bosatsu|Kannon Bosatsu]]. During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}’s government-ordered [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Buddhism from Shinto]], the temple became a Shinto shrine. In 1871, it was officially designated one of the '' [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]'' (国幣小社), or 3rd Ranked national shrine under the {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|qid=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}} system until 1946.Bernstein, Andrew. [http://museweb02-pub.mse.jhu.edu/login?uri=/journals/monumenta_nipponica/v063/63.1.bernstein.html "Whose Fuji?: Religion, Region, and State in the Fight for a National Symbol,"]{{dead link|date=November 2017 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }} ''Monumenta Nipponica,'' Vol. 63, No. 1, Spring 2008, pp. 51-99; Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' ==Notable structures== Many of the structures of Iwakiyama Shrine date from the early {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, and were built in 1694 under the sponsorship of the {{ill|Tsugaru clan|en|Tsugaru clan|qid=Q1062437|lt=Tsugaru clan|1=Tsugaru clan}} of {{ill|Hirosaki Domain|en|Hirosaki Domain|qid=Q903555|lt=Hirosaki Domain|1=Hirosaki Domain}}. The two-story main gate (''Ryōmon'') was built in 1628. The ''[[Honden]]'', ''[[Heiden (Shinto)|Heiden]]'', ''Oku-no-mon'' and Ryōmon are built in the ''{{ill|Yosegi|en|Yosegi|qid=Q15894888|lt=yosegi-zukuri|1=Yosegi-zaiku}}'' style with decorative wood carvings, which have given the shrine its nickname of “Oku-Nikko” after the more famous structures of the {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|qid=Q696641|lt=Nikkō Tōshō-gū|1=Nikkō Tōshō-gū}}. All of these buildings are registered as National {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|qid=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Properties|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}. ==See also== * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|qid=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines|1=List of Shinto shrines}} * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 3994492] * Graham, Patricia J. ''Faith and Power in Japanese Buddhist Art, 1600-2005''. University of Hawaii Press (2008) {{ISBN|0-8248-3191-8}} ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category|Iwakiyama shrine}} *[https://web.archive.org/web/20100107182114/http://www.aomori-jinjacho.or.jp/koukoku/iwakiyamajinja.html Aomori Jinja Honcho Official site of the shrine] *[http://www.pref.aomori.lg.jp/culture/jubun_kenzoubutu/02.html Aomori Prefectural site] *[http://www.worldeventsguide.com/event/1356/Aomori-Japan/Oyama-Mairi.html Oyama-mairi]{{Dead link|date=November 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }} {{Hiyoshi Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ [[en:Iwakiyama Shrine]] {{Commons category|Iwakiyama shrine}} [[fr:Iwakiyama-jinja]] [[ja:岩木山神社]] [[ko:이와키 산 신사]] [[mr:इवाकियामा जिंजा]] {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Iwakiyama Shrine |image = [[File:Iwakiyama_Shrine_haiden2.jpeg|270px|Middle Gate and Worship Hall]]
Middle Gate and Worship Hall |location = {{ill|Hirosaki|en|Hirosaki|qid=Q194087|lt=Hirosaki|1=Hirosaki}}, Aomori Prefecture, Japan |coordinates = {{Coord|40|37|18.8|N|140|20|26.2|E|region:JP-02_type:landmark_scale:25000|display=inline,title}} |deity = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|顕国魂神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}
{{ill|Tatsuhime-no-Kami|ja|湍津姫神|多都比姫神|1=Tagitsuhime|2=ja|3=タギツヒメ|4=fr|5=Tagitsuhime|6=zh|7=多岐都比賣命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatsuhime-no-Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|qq=}}
Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami
{{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山祇神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オオヤマツミ}}
{{ill|Sakanoue no Karitamaro|en|Sakanoue no Karitamaro|qid=Q3137722|lt=Sakanoue no Karitamaro|1=Sakanoue no Karitamaro}} |Rank = Former {{ill|Kokushi|en|Kokushi|qid=Q16801292|lt=National Minor Shrine|1=Kokushi}}
{{ill|Beppyo shrine|en|Beppyo shrine|qid=Q10898274|lt=Beppyo Shrine|1=Beppyo shrine}} |Established = {{ill|Hōki|en|Hōki|qid=Q18520676|lt=Hōki|1=Hōki}} 11 (780) |Main Hall = Three-bay {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|en|Nagare-zukuri|qid=Q210684|lt=Nagare-zukuri|1=Nagare-zukuri}} style, copper roof |Festivals = {{ill|Hassaku|lt=Hassaku|lt=Hassaku Festival|ja|八朔祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hassaku Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八朔}} (lunar August 1) |Map = {{location map|Japan Aomori|lat_deg=40|lat_min=37|lat_sec=18.8|lon_deg=140|lon_min=20|lon_sec=26.2|label=Iwakiyama Shrine|position=center|width=200|float=center|caption=}} }} '''Iwakiyama Shrine''' (岩木山神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located at the southeastern base of {{ill|Mount Iwaki|en|Mount Iwaki|qid=Q713857|lt=Mount Iwaki|1=Mount Iwaki}} in {{ill|Hirosaki|en|Hirosaki|qid=Q194087|lt=Hirosaki|1=Hirosaki}}, Aomori Prefecture, Japan. Known by locals as "Oyama" or "Oku Nikko," it is regarded as the Ichinomiya of {{ill|Tsugaru region|ja|津軽地方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsugaru region|qq=}}. The shrine was formerly classified as a {{ill|Kokushi|en|Kokushi|qid=Q16801292|lt=National Minor Shrine|1=Kokushi}}. == Overview == The shrine has long been revered as a guardian deity for agriculture, fishing, and Tsugaru's pioneering spirit. It also serves as a local place of ancestral worship.{{Cite web|title="Iwakiyama Shrine" - Aomori Prefecture Shrine Association|url=http://www.aomori-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja/chuko/jinja/70.htm|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20041230183615/http://www.aomori-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja/chuko/jinja/70.htm|archiveservice=Internet Archive|archivedate=2004-12-30|url-status=dead|url-status-date=2018-09-14}} The shrine’s approach doubles as a hiking trail to Mount Iwaki's summit, where its Okunomiya (inner shrine) is located. The shrine's design reflects the syncretic Shinto-Buddhism practices of the Kamakura period, featuring intricate carvings reminiscent of the Edo period’s {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|qid=Q696641|lt=Nikkō Tōshō-gū|1=Nikkō Tōshō-gū}}.{{Cite web|title=Iwakiyama Shrine - Hirosaki City Promotion|publisher=Hirosaki City|date=2018-6-15|url=http://www.city.hirosaki.aomori.jp/city_promotion/topics/2018/06/180615.html|accessdate=2018-9-14}} == Enshrined Deities == The shrine collectively refers to its five enshrined deities as "Iwakiyama Ōkami": * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|顕国魂神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} (Utsushikunidama-no-Kami) * {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|湍津姫神|多都比姫神|1=Tagitsuhime|2=ja|3=タギツヒメ|4=fr|5=Tagitsuhime|6=zh|7=多岐都比賣命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|13=User:Immanuelle/Tagitsuhime|qq=}} * Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami * {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山祇神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オオヤマツミ}} * {{ill|Sakanoue no Karitamaro|en|Sakanoue no Karitamaro|qid=Q3137722|lt=Sakanoue no Karitamaro|1=Sakanoue no Karitamaro}}{{Cite web|title=Oyama Pilgrimage - Iwakisan Tourism Association|url=http://www.iwakisan.com/oyama-sankei/2016/12/9/|accessdate=2018-9-14|publisher=Iwakisan Tourism Association}} == History == The shrine’s origins trace back to {{ill|Hōki|en|Hōki|qid=Q18520676|lt=Hōki|1=Hōki}} 11 (780), when the first structures were built on Mount Iwaki’s summit. In 800, {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|en|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|qid=Q941979|lt=Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|1=Sakanoue no Tamuramaro}} rebuilt the summit shrine after successfully pacifying the region. He also established the Orinomori Shrine (current Genkiman Shrine) at the mountain’s base. In 1091, the shrine was relocated to its present site in Hyakuzawa and renamed "Hyakutaku-ji," blending Shinto and {{ill|Mikkyō|en|Mikkyō|qid=Q336102|lt=Esoteric Buddhism|1=Mikkyō}} practices. It gained prominence under the support of local feudal lords during the Edo period. The shrine was reorganized under Meiji-era Shinto policies in 1873, achieving National Minor Shrine status. == Cultural Heritage == === National Important Cultural Properties === * Main Shrine Complex (Main Hall, Inner Gate, Sacred Fence, and Middle Gate) - Designated in 1971. * Worship Hall - Designated in 1908. * Tower Gate - Designated in 1908. === National Intangible Folk Cultural Properties === * Oyama Pilgrimage - Designated in 1984.{{Cite web|title=Iwakiyama Pilgrimage Event - Cultural Heritage Database|url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/302/5|accessdate=2022-07-14|publisher=Cultural Heritage Database|language=ja}} === Aomori Prefecture Designated Treasures === * Shrine Office Building - Designated in 2011.{{Cite web|title=Iwakiyama Shrine Office|url=https://www.pref.aomori.lg.jp/soshiki/kyoiku/e-bunka/juho_kenzoubutu_41.html|accessdate=2022-07-14|publisher=Aomori Prefecture Cultural Asset Protection Section}} == Festivals == * {{ill|Hassaku|lt=Hassaku|lt=Hassaku Festival|ja|八朔祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hassaku Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八朔}} (lunar August 1st) * Oyama Pilgrimage (Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property) == Access == * From {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|en|East Japan Railway Company|qid=Q499071|lt=JR East|1=East Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Ōu Main Line|en|Ōu Main Line|qid=Q386495|lt=Ōu Main Line|1=Ōu Main Line}} {{ill|Hirosaki Station|en|Hirosaki Station|qid=Q5770722|lt=Hirosaki Station|1=Hirosaki Station}} (Central Exit), take a {{ill|Kōnan Bus Company|en|Kōnan Bus Company|qid=Q6455226|lt=Kōnan Bus|1=Kōnan Bus Company}} bound for Karekinodaira for about 40 minutes. Alight at the Iwakiyama Shrine bus stop. == See Also == * {{ill|Mount Iwaki|en|Mount Iwaki|qid=Q713857|lt=Mount Iwaki|1=Mount Iwaki}} * {{ill|Japanese battleship Mutsu|en|Japanese battleship Mutsu|qid=Q1813237|lt=Japanese battleship Mutsu|1=Japanese battleship Mutsu}} - A Japanese battleship that enshrined a branch of Iwakiyama Shrine. == References == == External Links == * [https://iwakiyamajinja.or.jp/ Official Website of Iwakiyama Shrine] * [https://aomori-tourism.com/spot/detail_19.html Iwakiyama Shrine - Aomori Tourism Information] {{shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|岩木山神社|version=103235007|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:岩木山神社]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Commons category|Iwakiyama shrine}} [[en:Iwakiyama Shrine]] [[fr:Iwakiyama-jinja]] [[ko:이와키 산 신사]] [[mr:इवाकियामा जिंजा]] {{wikidata link|Q871375}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Hirosaki, Aomori]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] [[Category:Edo period architecture]] [[Category:History of Hirosaki, Aomori]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties in Hirosaki City]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Aomori Prefecture]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
